title: Entropy Signatures of Collective Modes and Vortex Dynamics in Rotating Two--Dimensional Bose--Einstein Condensates
authors: L. A. Machado, N. D. Chavda, B. Chatterjee, M. A. Caracanhas, B. Chakrabarti, A. Gammal, R. P. Sagar
abstract: We investigate the nonequilibrium dynamics of a two-dimensional rotating Bose gas confined in a symmetric anharmonic trap, employing the multiconfigurational time-dependent Hartree method for bosons (MCTDHB). We study states ranging from vortex-free configurations to multicharged (giant) vortices, prepared by tuning the rotation frequency, and analyze their response to sudden interaction and trap quenches. In vortex-free states, interaction quenches induce regular breathing--like dynamics, whereas in the presence of giant vortices they lead to symmetry-breaking surface excitations. In contrast, trap deformations that excite quadrupole-like modes produce stable oscillations in vortex-free condensates but trigger rapid, irregular, and effectively chaotic splitting dynamics in multicharged vortices. To characterize these processes beyond conventional density and phase observables, we employ information-theoretic measures, including marginal and joint entropies, mutual information, and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, supplemented by an angular-resolved KL measure that captures symmetry breaking and azimuthal localization. We find that chaotic splitting is accompanied by a pronounced growth of information-theoretic indicators, signaling the buildup of many-body correlations and increasing complexity in the system dynamics. Our results demonstrate the extreme sensitivity of giant vortices to excitation protocols and establish information-theoretic measures as a powerful framework to quantify correlations and complexity in rotating quantum gases.
summary: This paper explores how rotating 2D Bose-Einstein condensates respond to sudden changes in their environment. It demonstrates that while low-rotation states undergo regular oscillations, high-rotation 'giant' vortices exhibit chaotic splitting and fragmentation. By using information-theoretic tools like mutual information, the authors can precisely identify the transition between regular and chaotic dynamical regimes. This work establishes entropy-based measures as sensitive probes for many-body correlations in quantum gases.
main_problem: Investigating the nonequilibrium dynamics, stability, and symmetry breaking of rotating 2D Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) following interaction and trap quenches.
main_result: The study identifies a transition from regular, periodic oscillations in vortex-free states to irregular, chaotic dynamics in giant vortices, using mutual information to detect a clear dynamical phase transition at a specific rotation frequency.
method: The Multiconfigurational Time-Dependent Hartree method for bosons (MCTDHB) is used to solve the time-dependent Schrödinger equation for a fully correlated many-body system.
model_or_system: A 2D rotating Bose gas of N=8 identical bosons in a symmetric anharmonic (quartic) trap with a finite-range Gaussian interaction potential.
key_observables: One-body density and phase, Shannon entropies (marginal and joint), mutual information, Kullback-Leibler divergence (including angular-resolved), and angular Fourier components.
important_parameters: Rotation frequency (Omega), interaction strength (g), trap anisotropy, and number of bosons (N=8).
main_assumptions: The system is treated in a 2D plane with a finite-range Gaussian potential instead of a pure delta-function contact interaction; the number of orbitals (M) is finite.
figures_summary: Figures show density snapshots of vortex formation and fragmentation, time evolution of entropy and mutual information, and a 3D dynamical phase diagram of correlations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and Hamiltonian, describes the MCTDHB method, analyzes initial state characterization, explores dynamics under interaction quenches, examines dynamics under trap quenches, and concludes with a phase diagram of correlations.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful framework for using information-theoretic measures to quantify many-body complexity, fragmentation, and the onset of chaos in ultracold atomic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Initial condensate states at selected rotation frequen- cies Ω. Top panels show the density n(x, y; 0), and bottom panels display the corresponding phase. For Ω≤1.2, the condensate forms a uniform central cloud with no vortex, and the phase is essentially uniform. At Ω= 1.3, a central vortex emerges, while Ω= 1.5 and Ω= 2.0 show increasing vortex charge, evolving into a giant vortex with annular density and a phase indicating total charges of 4 and 8, respectively.

FIG. 2. Shannon information entropies and mutual informa- tion as a function of rotation frequency Ω. All four quantities, Sx, Sy, Sxy, and mutual information I, remain constant for Ω≤1.2. Beyond Ω= 1.2, each measure exhibits a kink and increases in a nonlinear manner, signaling the onset of cen- tral vortex formation and the emergence of correlations not directly visible in the density or phase profiles.

FIG. 3. Density evolution of the condensate for Ω= 0.5 fol- lowing an weak interaction quench. Snapshots at selected time points show the periodic expansion and contraction of the central bright cloud, characteristic of the monopole (breathing) mode.

FIG. 4. Time evolution of information-theoretic measures for Ω= 0.5 during the monopole dynamics. We follow the same quench protocol as in Fig. 3. Panels show (a) Sx, (b) Sy, (c) Sxy, and (d) mutual information I as functions of time.

FIG. 5. Time evolution of the density for the Ω= 2.0 conden- sate following a stronger interaction quench (g′ ≈5g). The central giant vortex (dark circular region) remains largely cir- cular, while the bright outer ring develops a fourfold modula- tion, splitting into four partially connected high-density seg- ments. The snapshots illustrate the combined effects of radial breathing and angular symmetry breaking at selected times, highlighting the dynamic fragmentation of the outer annular region.

FIG. 6. Time evolution of angular observables along the outer ring. We follow the same quench protocol as in Fig. 5. Ob- servables: (a) Radially weighted fourfold operator O4(t). (b) Angular Fourier components A4(t) and A2(t). (c) Angular Kullback–Leibler divergence Dθ KL(t) and (d) mutual infor- mation I(t).

FIG. 8. Time evolution of quadrupole and information- theoretic observables for Ω= 0.5 following the trap quench. (a) Quadrupole operator OQ(t), (b) marginal entropies Sx(t) (red curve) and Sy(t) (blue curve), (c) joint entropy Sxy(t), and (d) mutual information I(t)

FIG. 7. Density snapshots at selected times for Ω= 0.5 fol- lowing the trap quench. The initially circular, vortex-free cloud oscillates between prolate and oblate shapes along x and y axes. The density remains compact with no fragmentation, and the center of mass stays near the trap origin, indicating a nearly pure quadrupole oscillation.

FIG. 9. One-body density snapshots at selected times for the giant vortex with initial Ω= 2.0 following the trap quench along the x-axis. The initial giant vortex at the center and the thin outer ring exhibit strong deformation and fragmentation over time, leading to irregular, chaotic dynamics.

FIG. 10. Time evolution of collective and angular observables for Ω= 2.0 following the trap quench. Panels (a)–(c) show the monopole OM(t), quadrupole OQ(t), and fourfold mode O4(t), respectively. Panel (d) displays the angular Fourier modes A4(t) ,A2(t) and A1(t). All observables exhibit irregu- lar, large-amplitude oscillations indicative of chaotic dynam- ics.
